blocks.content: Module BI1
Lakehouse & Warehouse for BI Consumers
Understanding the Data Layer Before You Build Reports
Estimated Time: 60 Minutes

Module Objective: Understand the data layer from a BI developer’s perspective — what tables are available, how Gold-layer star schemas are structured, how DirectLake connects semantic models to Delta tables, how to identify good tables for reporting, how to read the data engineering team’s work, and what to ask for when the data does not meet your needs.
Welcome to Module BI1

Modules F1 and F2 taught you that Fabric is a unified platform with one storage layer (OneLake) and that every table is a Delta table. This module bridges the gap between the data engineering team’s work and your work as a BI developer.

You do not build Lakehouses, write PySpark, or run Pipelines. Data engineers do that. They land raw meter readings into Bronze tables, cleanse and deduplicate them into Silver tables, and model them into Gold-layer star schemas with fact tables, dimension tables, and aggregate views. Your job begins where theirs ends: you connect a semantic model to the Gold layer and build reports that answer business questions.

But you need to understand what you are connecting to. A BI developer who does not understand star schemas, grain definitions, or SCD Type 2 dimensions will build reports that produce wrong numbers. A developer who does not know about DirectLake mode will create slow reports that frustrate users. This module gives you the data layer literacy you need.

This module covers: the data layer architecture (Bronze/Silver/Gold from a BI lens), what Gold-layer tables look like in the energy domain, star schema anatomy (facts, dimensions, grain, surrogate keys), how DirectLake connects your semantic model to Delta tables, the default semantic model and when to customize it, Lakehouse SQL endpoint vs. Warehouse as data sources, how to evaluate data readiness for reporting, and what to communicate to the engineering team.
Part 1: The Data Layer — Bronze, Silver, Gold for BI Developers
The energy platform has three data layers. As a BI developer, you care about one of them. Understanding all three helps you know why.
BRONZE (lh_bronze_grid)            ← Raw data. Do not report on this.
│  raw_meter_readings: 50M rows, duplicates, strings, nulls
│  raw_scada_telemetry: sensor noise, missing values
SILVER (lh_silver_grid)            ← Cleansed. Data scientists use this.
│  meters: deduplicated, typed, validated. Still normalized.
│  scada_telemetry: clean sensor data
GOLD (lh_gold_grid or wh_grid_analytics)  ← YOUR DATA. Report on this.
fact_meter_readings: star schema fact with surrogate keys
dim_substations: descriptive attributes, SCD Type 2
dim_meters: meter attributes, tariff codes
dim_date: calendar dimension (year, quarter, month, day)
dim_tariffs: tariff codes and rates
v_monthly_grid_summary: pre-aggregated for dashboards
v_substation_health: asset management view

Rule: always connect your semantic model to Gold-layer tables. Gold tables are deduplicated, typed, validated, and modeled as star schemas. They are designed for your use case. Bronze and Silver tables contain raw or intermediate data that will produce incorrect numbers in reports (duplicates inflate totals, raw strings break visualizations, nulls create blank rows).
Why This Matters for Report Accuracy

If you connect a report to the Bronze raw_meter_readings table instead of the Gold fact_meter_readings table, you will see duplicate rows (the same meter reading loaded twice from overlapping SFTP exports), string columns where numbers belong (daily_kwh as VARCHAR), null regions that should be ‘UNKNOWN’, and no surrogate keys for joining to dimension tables. Every total will be wrong, every slicer will have blanks, and every stakeholder will lose trust in the report. The Gold layer exists to prevent this.
Part 2: Star Schema Anatomy for BI Developers
The Gold layer is organized as a star schema. Understanding its structure is essential for building correct semantic models.
The Energy Grid Star Schema
dim_date                        dim_substations
(date_key PK,                   (substation_sk PK,
calendar_date,                  substation_id,
year, quarter, month,           substation_name,
day_of_week, is_weekend)        voltage_kv, region,
│                        voltage_class,
│                        is_current)
│       fact_meter_readings          │
│  meter_sk FK → dim_meters         │
│  substation_sk FK → dim_substations│
│  date_key FK → dim_date            │
│  daily_kwh (measure)               │
│  peak_kw (measure)                  │
│  estimated_cost_eur (measure)       │
│  consumption_band (attribute)       │
dim_meters           dim_tariffs
(meter_sk PK,        (tariff_code PK,
meter_id,            description,
meter_type,          rate_per_kwh,
tariff_code,         effective_date)
is_current)
Key Concepts for BI Developers

Fact table — Contains measurable events. One row per meter per day. The numeric columns (daily_kwh, peak_kw, estimated_cost_eur) are what your DAX measures will aggregate. The foreign keys (meter_sk, substation_sk, date_key) are how you join to dimensions.

Dimension tables — Contain descriptive attributes for filtering and grouping. When a user clicks ‘NORTH’ in a region slicer, the filter flows from dim_substations through the relationship to fact_meter_readings, restricting the fact rows. Dimensions are the “slicers and axes” of your reports.

Grain — What one row in the fact table represents. For fact_meter_readings, the grain is “one row per meter per day.” Every DAX aggregation operates at this grain. If you misunderstand the grain, your SUM(daily_kwh) will be wrong.

Surrogate keys — Integer keys (meter_sk, substation_sk, date_key) generated by the engineering team. They replace business keys (meter_id, substation_id, reading_date) in the fact table. You use surrogate keys for relationships in your semantic model, not business keys.

SCD Type 2 — dim_substations has an is_current column. Multiple rows may exist for the same substation_id (one for each historical version). The fact table’s substation_sk points to the specific version that was current when the reading was taken. Your relationship should use substation_sk, not substation_id.
Part 3: DirectLake — The Connection Mode You Should Use

DirectLake is Fabric’s native connection mode for semantic models. It reads Delta Parquet files directly from OneLake into the VertiPaq in-memory engine. No data copy, no scheduled refresh, near-real-time freshness.
How DirectLake Works for Your Reports
No refresh to configure — When the engineering pipeline writes new meter readings to the Gold fact table at 02:30 AM, your 02:31 AM dashboard query sees the new data. No refresh schedule needed.

Fast queries — DirectLake reads columnar Parquet files directly into VertiPaq. Performance is nearly identical to Import mode for typical report interactions (slicer clicks, drill-through, page navigation).

Automatic framing — The engine periodically takes a snapshot of the Delta table’s current state. This determines which Parquet files are read. You can trigger framing manually by refreshing the semantic model.
Fallback — If a table exceeds the VertiPaq cache for your capacity SKU, DirectLake falls back to DirectQuery for that query. The report still works, just slower. Monitor for this in production.
Creating a DirectLake Semantic Model
•  Open the Gold Lakehouse (lh_gold_grid) or Warehouse (wh_grid_analytics) in the Fabric portal.
•  Click “New semantic model”.
•  Select the tables: fact_meter_readings, dim_substations, dim_meters, dim_date, dim_tariffs.
•  The model is created in DirectLake mode automatically.
•  Open the model in the web modeler. Define relationships, measures, and hierarchies (Module BI2).

Important: DirectLake only works with tables from a single Lakehouse or Warehouse. If your Gold layer spans multiple Lakehouses, consider using shortcuts to unify them under one Lakehouse, or use a Warehouse that cross-queries multiple sources.
Part 4: Default vs. Custom Semantic Models

Every Lakehouse and Warehouse automatically generates a default semantic model. It includes all tables and is already in DirectLake mode. But it has no relationships, no measures, and no security. It is a starting point, not a production model.

Rule: Use the default semantic model for quick data exploration. Create a custom semantic model for any report that will be shared with stakeholders. The custom model is where you define relationships, write DAX measures, implement security, and curate the analyst experience.
Part 5: Lakehouse vs. Warehouse as Your Data Source

From a BI developer’s perspective, it does not matter whether the Gold layer lives in a Lakehouse or a Warehouse. Both produce Delta tables in OneLake. Both support DirectLake. Both appear as tables in your semantic model. The choice is made by the engineering team based on their skills. You connect to whichever item they build.
Part 6: Evaluating Data Readiness for Reporting
Before building a semantic model, evaluate whether the Gold-layer data is ready for reporting. Use this checklist:

If any check fails, raise it with the engineering team. Data readiness issues are fixed in the pipeline (Bronze/Silver/Gold), not in the semantic model. A DAX workaround for missing data types or duplicates is fragile and wrong.
What to Ask the Engineering Team
•  What is the grain of each fact table? (One row per meter per day? Per hour? Per event?)
•  Which dimension tables have SCD Type 2? How do I identify the current version? (is_current = 1?)
•  When does the pipeline run? When is the Gold layer refreshed? (So I know when my DirectLake model will show new data.)
•  Are there pre-aggregated views or tables for dashboard performance? (v_monthly_grid_summary?)
•  What naming convention do tables follow? (gold.fact_*, gold.dim_*, gold.v_*?)
•  Are there any known data quality issues I should be aware of?
•  Is Row-Level Security already implemented at the data layer, or do I need to implement it in the semantic model?
Module BI1 Checkpoint
You now understand:
•  The data layer from a BI perspective — Bronze (raw, do not report on), Silver (cleansed, for data scientists), Gold (star schema, YOUR data for reports)

•  Star schema anatomy — fact tables (measurable events with foreign keys), dimension tables (descriptive attributes for slicers), grain (what one row represents), surrogate keys (integer keys for relationships), SCD Type 2 (is_current for historical dimensions)
•  DirectLake — the recommended connection mode: reads Delta from OneLake into VertiPaq, no refresh needed, near-import speed, fallback to DirectQuery for oversized tables
•  Default vs. custom semantic models — default for exploration (no relationships, no measures), custom for production (star schema relationships, DAX measures, RLS, hierarchies)
•  Lakehouse vs. Warehouse as BI sources — both produce Delta tables, both support DirectLake, the choice is the engineering team’s; you connect to whichever Gold layer they build
•  Data readiness evaluation — 9-point checklist (star schema, grain, surrogate keys, dimension completeness, date dimension, data types, duplicates, freshness, documentation)
•  Communicating with the engineering team — 7 questions to ask before building a semantic model
Hands-On Lab
•  Open the Gold Lakehouse or Warehouse in your energy workspace. Navigate to the Tables/ section (or the gold schema). List all available tables.
•  Identify the fact table(s) and dimension table(s). State the grain of each fact table in one sentence.
•  Open the SQL analytics endpoint. Write a quick query to verify the fact table has surrogate keys: SELECT TOP 10 meter_sk, substation_sk, date_key, daily_kwh FROM gold.fact_meter_readings.
•  Verify dimension completeness: query dim_substations and check for substation_name, region, voltage_class, is_current columns.
•  Check for duplicates: SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(DISTINCT CONCAT(meter_sk, date_key)) FROM gold.fact_meter_readings. If they differ, flag it.
•  Open the default semantic model for the Lakehouse or Warehouse. Note: no relationships, no measures, no security. This is why you will build a custom model in Module BI2.
•  Write down 3 questions you would ask the engineering team based on the data readiness checklist.
You are not just connecting to tables.

You are reading the data engineering team’s work — understanding their star schema design, their grain decisions, their SCD patterns — so that your semantic model, DAX measures, and reports produce correct numbers. The BI developer who understands the data layer builds reports that stakeholders trust. The one who does not builds reports that produce wrong totals and blame the data.

In Module BI2, we build the semantic model — creating a DirectLake-connected model with star schema relationships, hierarchies, display folders, and the foundation that all DAX measures and reports will be built on. This is where your data layer knowledge becomes a production analytical engine.

chapters.title: Semantic Models & DirectLake
chapters.description: Building semantic models and leveraging DirectLake mode for optimal query performance

blocks.content: Module BI2
Semantic Models & DirectLake
Building the Analytical Engine for Energy Reporting
Estimated Time: 120 Minutes

Module Objective: Build a production-quality semantic model for energy grid analytics — connect via DirectLake to Gold-layer Delta tables, define star schema relationships with correct cardinality and filter direction, create role-playing dimensions for date analysis, build hierarchies for drill-down navigation, organize with display folders, introduce measures vs. calculated columns, and optimize the model for VertiPaq performance.
Welcome to Module BI2
Module BI1 taught you to read the data layer: Gold star schemas, fact tables, dimensions, surrogate keys, grain. Now you build the analytical engine that sits on top of that data layer.

The semantic model is the brain of every Power BI report. It is an in-memory analytical model that contains tables, relationships between tables, business logic (DAX measures), security rules, and formatting. Reports do not query Delta tables directly — they query the semantic model. The model defines how tables relate, how filters propagate, what calculations are available, and who can see what.

A well-built semantic model makes every downstream report fast, correct, and easy to build. A poorly-built model — wrong relationships, missing measures, no hierarchies — forces report developers to work around structural problems in every visual.

This module covers: what a semantic model is and what it contains, connecting to Gold-layer tables via DirectLake, defining star schema relationships, role-playing dimensions for date analysis, hierarchies for drill-down, display folders for organization, measures vs. calculated columns, the VertiPaq engine, and model optimization.
Part 1: What Is a Semantic Model?

A semantic model (formerly called a “dataset”) is an in-memory analytical data model powered by the VertiPaq columnar engine. It is the layer between raw data storage (Lakehouse, Warehouse) and visual reports.
What the Semantic Model Contains
Tables — References to your Gold-layer Delta tables. In DirectLake mode, the model reads Parquet files directly from OneLake into the VertiPaq cache.
Relationships — Join definitions between tables (one-to-many, many-to-many). These determine how filters propagate when a user clicks a slicer, selects a bar, or applies a filter.

Measures — DAX formulas that calculate business metrics (Total Consumption, Revenue, YoY Growth). Measures are evaluated dynamically based on the current filter context. They are the core business logic.
Calculated columns — DAX-computed columns evaluated at refresh time and stored in the model. Use sparingly — prefer source-level columns from the engineering pipeline.
Hierarchies — Drill-down paths: Year → Quarter → Month → Day, or Region → Substation. Enable intuitive exploration in reports.
Display folders — Organizational folders in the Fields pane: group measures by business domain (Consumption, Cost, Time Intelligence, Geography) so report developers can find what they need.
Row-Level Security — DAX filter rules that restrict which rows a user sees based on their identity. Covered in depth in Module BI6.
The VertiPaq Engine

Semantic models are powered by the VertiPaq columnar engine — the same engine behind SQL Server Analysis Services and Azure Analysis Services. VertiPaq compresses data aggressively using dictionary encoding (few-unique-value columns like region, status) and value encoding (sequential integers like date_key, meter_sk). It evaluates DAX queries at interactive speeds: a typical slicer click resolves in under 100 milliseconds on a well-modeled dataset.
Part 2: Creating a DirectLake Semantic Model
Step-by-Step Creation
•  Open the Gold Lakehouse (lh_gold_grid) or Warehouse (wh_grid_analytics) in the Fabric portal.
•  Click “New semantic model” from the toolbar.
•  Name the model: sm_grid_analytics.
•  Select the tables to include. For the energy grid star schema, select: fact_meter_readings, dim_substations, dim_meters, dim_date, dim_tariffs.
•  Do NOT include staging or silver tables — only Gold-layer tables belong in the semantic model.
•  Click Create. The model is created in DirectLake mode automatically.
•  The web-based model editor opens, showing a diagram view of your tables.
DirectLake Behavior
What NOT to Include in the Model
•  Staging/Bronze tables — raw data with duplicates and wrong types.
•  Silver tables — intermediate data not modeled as star schema.
•  Audit/log tables — operational metadata, not analytical.
•  Wide flat tables — denormalized exports with 100+ columns. If this is all the engineers provide, ask for a proper star schema.
•  High-cardinality text columns — free-text descriptions, GUIDs, or raw addresses compress poorly and bloat VertiPaq memory.
Part 3: Defining Star Schema Relationships

Relationships are the skeleton of the semantic model. They determine how filters flow between tables when a user interacts with a report. Incorrect relationships produce incorrect numbers. Getting relationships right is the single most important modeling task.
Energy Grid Relationships
Relationship Rules

Always use surrogate keys — Join on integer keys (meter_sk, substation_sk, date_key), not business keys (meter_id, substation_id). Surrogate keys are faster for VertiPaq and unambiguous for SCD Type 2 dimensions.

One-to-many from dimension to fact — The dimension table is the “one” side; the fact table is the “many” side. This is the star schema standard. If Power BI detects many-to-many, your data may have duplicates or you are joining on the wrong column.

Single cross-filter direction — Filters flow from dimension to fact (dimension → fact). When a user selects ‘NORTH’ in a region slicer on dim_substations, the filter flows to fact_meter_readings. This is the default and recommended direction.

Avoid bi-directional filtering — Bi-directional allows filters to flow fact → dimension, which creates ambiguity when multiple paths exist. Use it only when specifically needed (e.g., filtering a dimension to show only values that exist in the fact).
One active relationship per pair — Only one relationship can be active between two tables. Use inactive relationships with USERELATIONSHIP() in DAX for role-playing dimensions.
SCD Type 2 Relationship Handling

dim_substations has multiple rows per substation_id (one for each historical version). The fact table’s substation_sk points to the specific surrogate key of the version that was current when the reading was taken. Because you join on surrogate key (substation_sk), not business key (substation_id), the relationship is clean one-to-many with no ambiguity.
Important: Do not add a relationship between substation_id and anything. The surrogate key (substation_sk) is the only correct join column for SCD Type 2 dimensions in the semantic model.
Part 4: Role-Playing Dimensions

A role-playing dimension is a single dimension table used in multiple relationships with the same fact table. The most common example: a date dimension joining to multiple date columns in the fact table.
Energy Example: Reading Date vs. Load Date
The fact table may have two date-related keys:
•  date_key — the reading date (when the meter measurement was taken).
•  load_date_key — the pipeline load date (when the data entered the warehouse).
Both join to dim_date, but only one relationship can be active:
// Active relationship (default): dim_date.date_key <-> fact.date_key
// This is used by all measures by default
// Inactive relationship: dim_date.date_key <-> fact.load_date_key
// Activated explicitly in specific measures:
Consumption by Load Date =
CALCULATE(
[Total Consumption kWh],
USERELATIONSHIP(fact_meter_readings[load_date_key], dim_date[date_key])

When a user places dim_date columns on a visual axis, the active relationship (reading date) is used by default. The “Consumption by Load Date” measure explicitly activates the inactive relationship to analyze data by when it was loaded rather than when it was measured. This pattern is essential for data quality monitoring (detecting late-arriving meter readings).
Part 5: Hierarchies for Drill-Down Navigation

Hierarchies enable drill-down in visuals: a user starts at the year level, clicks to see quarters, clicks again for months, and again for days. Without hierarchies, users must manually drag columns onto visuals for each level.
Energy Grid Hierarchies
Creating Hierarchies in the Model Editor
•  In the diagram view, right-click the top-level column (calendar_year in dim_date) and select “Create hierarchy.”
•  Name it: Calendar.
•  Drag calendar_quarter, calendar_month, and calendar_date into the hierarchy in order.
•  Repeat for Geography (region → voltage_class → substation_name) and Meter Type.
In reports, users will see the hierarchy as a single field in the Fields pane. Dragging “Calendar” onto an axis automatically enables drill-down with the ↓ button on visuals.
Best Practices for Hierarchies
•  Place hierarchies on dimension tables, never on the fact table.
•  Use columns with natural ordering (year → quarter → month, not month → year).
•  Keep hierarchies to 3–4 levels. Deeper hierarchies confuse users.
•  Add a sort column if needed: month_name should sort by month_number (January = 1), not alphabetically (April = 1).
Part 6: Display Folders for Organization

Display folders organize measures and columns in the Fields pane. Without them, a model with 30 measures is a flat, unsorted list. With display folders, measures are grouped by business domain, making the model self-documenting for report developers.
Energy Grid Display Folder Structure
Setting Display Folders

In the web-based model editor, select a measure or column, then set its Display Folder property in the Properties pane. Type the folder name (e.g., “Consumption Measures”). All items with the same folder name appear together.
•  Use consistent naming: business domains (“Consumption,” “Cost,” “Time Intelligence”), not technical names (“DAX Measures Group 1”).

•  Hide columns that report developers should never use directly: surrogate keys (meter_sk, substation_sk, date_key) should be hidden. They are used for relationships but should not appear as slicer options.
•  Hide the fact table’s foreign key columns. Users should navigate via dimension attributes, not by dragging integer keys into visuals.
Part 7: Measures vs. Calculated Columns — When to Use Each
Starter Measures for the Energy Model
These measures will be developed fully in Module BI3 (DAX Deep Dive). Here is the preview to understand what lives in the semantic model:
// Basic aggregations
Total Consumption kWh = SUM(fact_meter_readings[daily_kwh])
Peak Demand kW = MAX(fact_meter_readings[peak_kw])
Total Cost EUR = SUM(fact_meter_readings[estimated_cost_eur])
Active Meters = DISTINCTCOUNT(fact_meter_readings[meter_sk])
Avg Daily kWh = DIVIDE([Total Consumption kWh], [Active Meters], 0)

Note: Always use DIVIDE() instead of the / operator. DIVIDE() handles division by zero gracefully (returns 0 or a specified alternate value). The / operator throws an error that breaks visuals. This is a non-negotiable DAX practice.
When to Use a Calculated Column
// Calculated column: consumption tier for slicing
// (only if not already in the Gold table as consumption_band)
Consumption Tier =
SWITCH(
TRUE(),
fact_meter_readings[daily_kwh] >= 500, "High",
fact_meter_readings[daily_kwh] >= 100, "Medium",
"Low"

Better approach: Ask the engineering team to add consumption_band as a column in the Gold fact table. Source-level columns are cheaper (no VertiPaq memory overhead from calculated columns), consistent across all semantic models, and maintained in one place. Calculated columns are a last resort when you cannot modify the source.
Part 8: Model Optimization for VertiPaq Performance
How VertiPaq Compresses Data
Column-oriented storage — Each column is stored independently. Unused columns are never read. Removing columns from the model directly reduces memory.

Dictionary encoding — Columns with few unique values (region with 4 values, status with 5 values) compress to tiny dictionaries. Columns with millions of unique values (free-text descriptions, GUIDs) compress poorly and bloat memory.
Value encoding — Sequential integers (date_key, meter_sk) use mathematical encoding that is extremely compact.
Optimization Checklist
Disabling Auto Date/Time
By default, Power BI creates a hidden date table for every column with a DATE data type. With 5 date columns across your model, that is 5 hidden tables consuming memory for no benefit. Disable this:
•  In Power BI Desktop: File → Options and settings → Options → Current File → Data Load → uncheck “Auto date/time.”
•  In the web modeler: this is controlled by the “Mark as date table” action on your dim_date.

After disabling auto date/time, mark your dim_date as the date table: right-click dim_date in the model editor, select “Mark as date table,” and choose the calendar_date column (the DATE column with no gaps).
Diagnosing Performance
Performance Analyzer — In Power BI Desktop, enable Performance Analyzer to measure how long each visual takes to render and which DAX queries are slow.
DAX Studio — An external tool that connects to the semantic model for direct DAX execution, query plan analysis, and VertiPaq storage profiling. Essential for serious optimization.
Model size in Fabric — Check the model’s memory consumption in the Fabric portal. Compare column sizes to identify bloated columns that need removal.
Part 9: The Complete Energy Grid Semantic Model
Here is the complete model specification for sm_grid_analytics:
sm_grid_analytics (DirectLake on wh_grid_analytics / lh_gold_grid)
├── TABLES:
│   fact_meter_readings (measure columns visible, FK columns hidden)
│   dim_date (marked as Date Table on calendar_date)
│   dim_substations (is_current hidden; used for filter only)
│   dim_meters (meter_sk hidden)
│   dim_tariffs
├── RELATIONSHIPS:
│   dim_date.date_key (1) → (*) fact_meter_readings.date_key  [ACTIVE]
│   dim_substations.substation_sk (1) → (*) fact.substation_sk  [ACTIVE]
│   dim_meters.meter_sk (1) → (*) fact.meter_sk  [ACTIVE]
│   dim_tariffs.tariff_code (1) → (*) dim_meters.tariff_code  [ACTIVE]
│   All: Single cross-filter direction
├── HIERARCHIES:
│   Calendar: year → quarter → month → date
│   Geography: region → voltage_class → substation_name
│   Meter Type: meter_type → tariff_code → meter_id
├── DISPLAY FOLDERS:
│   Consumption Measures, Cost Measures, Time Intelligence, Ranking
│   Calendar Attributes, Substation Attributes
├── STARTER MEASURES (expanded in Module BI3):
│   Total Consumption kWh, Peak Demand kW, Total Cost EUR,
│   Active Meters, Avg Daily kWh
└── OPTIMIZATION:
Auto date/time: DISABLED
dim_date: MARKED as date table
FK columns: HIDDEN (meter_sk, substation_sk, date_key)
Unused source columns: REMOVED
Module BI2 Checkpoint
You now understand:
•  What a semantic model is — in-memory analytical model with tables, relationships, measures, calculated columns, hierarchies, display folders, and RLS; powered by the VertiPaq columnar engine
•  DirectLake — creating a model from Gold Lakehouse/Warehouse, no refresh needed, automatic framing, fallback to DirectQuery, single-source constraint; 5-behavior table

•  Star schema relationships — 4 relationships for the energy grid (dim_date, dim_substations, dim_meters, dim_tariffs → fact); surrogate key joins, one-to-many cardinality, single filter direction, SCD Type 2 handling
•  Role-playing dimensions — active vs. inactive relationships for date analysis; USERELATIONSHIP in DAX to activate the inactive relationship for load date analysis
•  Hierarchies — Calendar (year → quarter → month → date), Geography (region → voltage_class → substation), Meter Type; creation steps, 3–4 level best practice, sort column for month names
•  Display folders — organizing measures by business domain (Consumption, Cost, Time Intelligence, Ranking); hiding FK columns; clean Fields pane for report developers
•  Measures vs. calculated columns — 6-point comparison table; measures for all business metrics (dynamic, zero memory), calculated columns as last resort; DIVIDE() as non-negotiable practice

•  Model optimization — 7-point checklist (remove unused columns, integer keys, hide FK columns, avoid high-cardinality text, prefer measures, disable auto date/time, mark dim_date as date table); VertiPaq compression mechanics; Performance Analyzer and DAX Studio
Hands-On Lab

•  Create a new semantic model (sm_grid_analytics) from your Gold Lakehouse or Warehouse in DirectLake mode. Include only Gold-layer tables: fact_meter_readings, dim_substations, dim_meters, dim_date, dim_tariffs.

•  Define 4 relationships: dim_date → fact (date_key), dim_substations → fact (substation_sk), dim_meters → fact (meter_sk), dim_tariffs → dim_meters (tariff_code). All one-to-many, single filter direction.
•  Create 3 hierarchies: Calendar (year → quarter → month → date), Geography (region → voltage_class → substation_name), Meter Type (meter_type → tariff_code).
•  Create display folders: “Consumption Measures,” “Cost Measures,” “Calendar Attributes,” “Substation Attributes.” Assign measures and columns to folders.
•  Hide all surrogate key columns (meter_sk, substation_sk, date_key) and the fact table’s FK columns. Verify they disappear from the Fields pane but relationships still work.
•  Write 5 starter measures: Total Consumption kWh, Peak Demand kW, Total Cost EUR, Active Meters, Avg Daily kWh (using DIVIDE). Place them in the Consumption Measures display folder.
•  Disable auto date/time. Mark dim_date as the date table using the calendar_date column.

•  Create a quick test report: drag the Calendar hierarchy onto the X axis and Total Consumption kWh onto the Y axis of a bar chart. Test drill-down from year to quarter to month. Verify the filter propagates correctly.
•  Verify DirectLake: check the model’s connection mode in settings. Confirm it shows DirectLake. Note the last-framed timestamp.

In Module BI3, we go deep into DAX — filter context and CALCULATE, ALL/ALLEXCEPT for percentage-of-total patterns, time intelligence (YTD, SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR, rolling 12-month, prior period), iterators (SUMX for weighted calculations), RANKX and TOPN, variables for readable formulas, and the complete energy measure library. DAX is the core skill of Power BI. This module gives it the depth it demands.

chapters.title: DAX Deep Dive
chapters.description: Mastering DAX formulas, calculations, and context for advanced data analysis

blocks.content: Module BI3
DAX Deep Dive
Think in Filters, Not in Rows
Estimated Time: 150 Minutes

Module Objective: Master DAX for energy analytics — understand filter context and how it drives every calculation, use CALCULATE to override and shape filter context, build percentage-of-total and filtered-metric patterns with ALL/ALLEXCEPT, implement time intelligence (YTD, same period last year, rolling 12-month, prior period), use iterators (SUMX) for weighted calculations, rank substations and meters with RANKX and TOPN, write readable formulas with VAR/RETURN, and build the complete energy measure library.
Welcome to Module BI3
Module BI2 built the semantic model: tables, relationships, hierarchies, display folders. The model is the structure. DAX is the logic.

DAX (Data Analysis Expressions) is the formula language for semantic models. Every number on every Power BI report — every total, every percentage, every comparison, every ranking — is the result of a DAX evaluation. The PySpark track has its DataFrame API. The T-SQL track has its window functions and stored procedures. The BI track has DAX. Mastering it is not optional.

The mental model: think in filters, not in rows. A DAX measure does not loop through rows. It defines what to calculate (SUM, COUNT, AVERAGE) and then the filter context determines which rows participate. When a user clicks ‘NORTH’ in a region slicer, the filter context changes, and every measure recalculates for NORTH — without the measure formula changing. Understanding filter context is the single key to DAX mastery.

This module covers: filter context and evaluation context, CALCULATE (the most important DAX function), ALL/ALLEXCEPT for percentage-of-total patterns, time intelligence (YTD, SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR, rolling 12-month, DATEADD), iterators (SUMX, AVERAGEX), RANKX and TOPN for ranking, SWITCH for dynamic measures, VAR/RETURN for readable formulas, performance considerations, and the complete energy measure library.
Part 1: Filter Context — The Foundation of DAX

Every DAX measure is evaluated within a filter context: the set of active filters at the point where the measure is calculated. The filter context is determined by report slicers, visual axes, page filters, row positions in a matrix, and CALCULATE modifiers.
How Filter Context Works
Consider this measure:
Total Consumption kWh = SUM(fact_meter_readings[daily_kwh])
The formula never changes. The result changes based on where the measure is evaluated:

The DAX formula does not change. The filter context changes. DAX automatically adapts. This is both the power and the complexity of DAX. Every measure you write must be correct in every possible filter context — not just the one you tested.
Part 2: CALCULATE — The Most Important DAX Function

CALCULATE evaluates an expression in a modified filter context. It takes the existing filter context, changes it according to filter arguments, and then evaluates the expression. Every advanced DAX pattern uses CALCULATE.
CALCULATE(
<expression>,     // The measure or aggregation to evaluate
<filter1>,        // Filter modifier 1
<filter2>,        // Filter modifier 2 (AND logic)
Pattern 1: Add a Filter
// Consumption for NORTH region only, regardless of any slicer
Consumption NORTH =
CALCULATE(
[Total Consumption kWh],
dim_substations[region] = "NORTH"
Pattern 2: Remove a Filter (Percentage of Total)
// What % of the grand total does the current filter represent?
Consumption % of Total =
DIVIDE(
[Total Consumption kWh],
CALCULATE([Total Consumption kWh], ALL(dim_substations[region]))
// ALL(dim_substations[region]) removes the region filter,
// giving the total across all regions as the denominator.
// If the user slices to NORTH, numerator = NORTH, denominator = all regions.
Pattern 3: Remove All Filters on a Table
// Consumption as % of the absolute grand total (no filters at all)
Consumption % of Grand Total =
DIVIDE(
[Total Consumption kWh],
CALCULATE([Total Consumption kWh], ALL(fact_meter_readings))
Pattern 4: Keep Specific Filters (ALLEXCEPT)
// Show each substation's consumption as % of its region total
// Remove all filters EXCEPT region
Consumption % of Region =
DIVIDE(
[Total Consumption kWh],
CALCULATE(
[Total Consumption kWh],
ALLEXCEPT(dim_substations, dim_substations[region])
Key Filter Modifier Functions
Part 3: Time Intelligence — Comparing Across Periods

Requirement: Time intelligence functions require a dedicated date table (dim_date) marked as the date table in the semantic model, with a DATE column containing no gaps and no duplicates. If your dim_date was set up correctly in BI2, you are ready.
Year-to-Date (YTD)
YTD Consumption kWh =
TOTALYTD(
[Total Consumption kWh],
dim_date[calendar_date]
// Cumulative consumption from Jan 1 to the current date in context.
// If the user is viewing March, YTD = Jan + Feb + Mar.
Same Period Last Year
Consumption LY =
CALCULATE(
[Total Consumption kWh],
SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(dim_date[calendar_date])
// Shifts the date filter back exactly one year.
// If viewing March 2026, evaluates for March 2025.
Year-over-Year Growth
YoY Growth % =
VAR CurrentPeriod = [Total Consumption kWh]
VAR LastYear = [Consumption LY]
RETURN
DIVIDE(CurrentPeriod - LastYear, LastYear, 0)
// Format as percentage in the model properties.
// Positive = growth, Negative = decline, 0 = no prior year data.
Month-to-Date and Quarter-to-Date
MTD Consumption kWh =
TOTALMTD([Total Consumption kWh], dim_date[calendar_date])
QTD Consumption kWh =
TOTALQTD([Total Consumption kWh], dim_date[calendar_date])
Rolling 12-Month Total
Rolling 12M Consumption =
CALCULATE(
[Total Consumption kWh],
DATESINPERIOD(
dim_date[calendar_date],
MAX(dim_date[calendar_date]),
MONTH
// Always shows the trailing 12-month total ending at the latest date
// in the current filter context. Smooths seasonality.
Previous Month and Month-over-Month
Consumption Previous Month =
CALCULATE(
[Total Consumption kWh],
DATEADD(dim_date[calendar_date], -1, MONTH)
MoM Growth % =
VAR Current = [Total Consumption kWh]
VAR Prior = [Consumption Previous Month]
RETURN
DIVIDE(Current - Prior, Prior, 0)
Time Intelligence Function Reference
Part 4: Iterators — SUMX, AVERAGEX, COUNTX
Iterators evaluate an expression row-by-row and then aggregate the results. They are essential when the calculation depends on values from multiple columns in the same row.
SUMX — Row-Level Calculation Then Sum
// Cost calculation: daily_kwh * rate_per_kwh per reading, then sum
// (use when estimated_cost_eur is not pre-calculated in Gold)
Total Calculated Cost =
SUMX(
fact_meter_readings,
fact_meter_readings[daily_kwh]
* RELATED(dim_tariffs[rate_per_kwh])
// RELATED() traverses the relationship from fact to dim_tariffs
// via dim_meters to get the tariff rate for each reading.
AVERAGEX — Weighted Average
// Weighted average tariff rate (weighted by consumption)
Weighted Avg Rate =
DIVIDE(
SUMX(
fact_meter_readings,
fact_meter_readings[daily_kwh]
* RELATED(dim_tariffs[rate_per_kwh])
[Total Consumption kWh]
When to Use Iterators vs. Simple Aggregations
Part 5: RANKX and TOPN — Ranking and Filtering
Ranking Substations by Consumption
Substation Rank =
RANKX(
ALL(dim_substations),
[Total Consumption kWh],
DESC,
DENSE
// ALL(dim_substations) creates the ranking scope:
// rank across ALL substations, not just those in the current filter.
// DESC = highest consumption = rank 1.
// DENSE = no gaps (1, 2, 3, not 1, 2, 4).
Top N Consumption
// Total consumption from the top 10 substations only
Top 10 Substation Consumption =
CALCULATE(
[Total Consumption kWh],
TOPN(
ALL(dim_substations),
[Total Consumption kWh],
DESC
// % of total from top 10 substations
Top 10 Concentration % =
DIVIDE([Top 10 Substation Consumption], [Total Consumption kWh])
Part 6: SWITCH — Dynamic Measures and Conditional Logic
Dynamic Metric Selector
Create a disconnected table (no relationships) that acts as a slicer for choosing which metric to display:
// Step 1: Create a disconnected table in the model
// MetricSelector = {"Consumption kWh", "Peak Demand kW", "Cost EUR", "Active Meters"}
// Step 2: Create the dynamic measure
Selected Metric =
SWITCH(
SELECTEDVALUE(MetricSelector[Metric]),
"Consumption kWh", [Total Consumption kWh],
"Peak Demand kW", [Peak Demand kW],
"Cost EUR", [Total Cost EUR],
"Active Meters", [Active Meters],
BLANK()
// Step 3: Place MetricSelector[Metric] as a slicer on the report.
// Place [Selected Metric] on the value axis of a chart.
// User picks the metric; the chart updates dynamically.
Conditional Formatting with SWITCH(TRUE())
// Color indicator for consumption levels
Consumption Status =
SWITCH(
TRUE(),
[YoY Growth %] > 0.10, "Red - Growing Fast",
[YoY Growth %] > 0, "Amber - Growing",
[YoY Growth %] <= 0, "Green - Stable or Declining",
"No Data"
Part 7: VAR / RETURN — Readable and Performant DAX
Variables store intermediate results. They are evaluated once and reused, improving both readability (name the intermediate step) and performance (no re-evaluation).
Complex Measure with Variables
// Grid efficiency: cost per kWh with period comparison
Grid Cost Efficiency =
VAR CurrentCost = [Total Cost EUR]
VAR CurrentConsumption = [Total Consumption kWh]
VAR CostPerKwh = DIVIDE(CurrentCost, CurrentConsumption, 0)
VAR PriorYearCost = [Consumption LY]  // Note: this should reference a cost LY measure
VAR PriorCostPerKwh = DIVIDE(
CALCULATE([Total Cost EUR], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(dim_date[calendar_date])),
[Consumption LY],
VAR Improvement = CostPerKwh - PriorCostPerKwh
RETURN
Improvement
// Negative = cost per kWh decreased (good).
// Positive = cost per kWh increased (investigate).

Rule: Always use VAR for any intermediate calculation. Variables are evaluated once and cached. Without VAR, the same sub-expression (like CALCULATE([Total Cost EUR], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(...))) would be evaluated multiple times in the same formula, wasting compute.
Debugging with Variables
When a measure returns an unexpected result, change RETURN to return each intermediate VAR one at a time. This isolates which step produces the wrong value:
// Debug step: change RETURN to inspect each variable
// RETURN CurrentCost          -- Is the cost correct?
// RETURN CurrentConsumption    -- Is consumption correct?
// RETURN CostPerKwh           -- Is the ratio correct?
// RETURN PriorCostPerKwh      -- Is the prior year correct?
// RETURN Improvement          -- Final result
Part 8: The Complete Energy Measure Library

This is the production measure library for the energy grid semantic model. Every measure uses energy domain terminology, follows DAX best practices (DIVIDE, VAR, explicit filter context), and is organized by display folder.
Part 9: DAX Performance Considerations
Module BI3 Checkpoint
You now have the complete DAX pattern library for energy analytics:
•  Filter context — how slicers, axes, and filters determine which rows participate in every measure evaluation; the same formula produces different results in different contexts

•  CALCULATE — the engine for modifying filter context; 4 patterns (add filter, remove filter with ALL, remove all filters, keep specific filters with ALLEXCEPT); 5 key modifier functions (ALL, ALLEXCEPT, REMOVEFILTERS, KEEPFILTERS, USERELATIONSHIP)

•  Time intelligence — TOTALYTD, TOTALMTD, TOTALQTD for cumulative periods; SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR for year-over-year; DATEADD for month-over-month; DATESINPERIOD for rolling 12-month; PARALLELPERIOD for complete prior periods; date table requirement
•  Iterators — SUMX for row-level calculations then sum (cost = kwh * rate per row); AVERAGEX for weighted averages; when to use iterators vs. simple aggregations
•  RANKX and TOPN — ranking substations by consumption (ALL for scope, DESC, DENSE); Top N concentration percentage
•  SWITCH — dynamic metric selector with disconnected slicer table; SWITCH(TRUE()) for conditional categorization
•  VAR/RETURN — readable and performant DAX; debugging by returning intermediate variables; non-negotiable practice for complex measures
•  Complete energy measure library — 18 measures across 5 display folders (Consumption, Cost, Time Intelligence, Comparison, Ranking)
•  Performance — 6 practices: VAR caching, DIVIDE safety, avoid unnecessary iterators, REMOVEFILTERS for clarity, pre-aggregate when possible, avoid calculated columns
Hands-On Lab

•  Open your sm_grid_analytics semantic model from BI2. Write all 5 Consumption measures (Total Consumption kWh, Peak Demand kW, Active Meters, Avg Daily kWh, High-Consumption Readings). Test each in a card visual.
•  Write the 3 Cost measures. Verify Avg Cost Per Meter = Total Cost EUR / Active Meters for a sample region.

•  Write CALCULATE measures: Consumption NORTH (add filter), Consumption % of Total (ALL), Consumption % of Region (ALLEXCEPT). Place in a matrix with regions on rows and verify percentages sum to 100%.

•  Verify your dim_date is marked as a date table. Write all 6 Time Intelligence measures: YTD, MTD, Consumption LY, YoY Growth %, Rolling 12M, MoM Growth %. Place in a line chart with Calendar hierarchy on the axis and verify drill-down works.
•  Write the SUMX iterator measure for Total Calculated Cost. Compare to Total Cost EUR (pre-calculated in Gold). They should match.
•  Write Substation Rank and Top 10 Concentration %. Place Substation Rank in a table visual with substation_name. Verify rank 1 is the highest consumer.
•  Create a disconnected MetricSelector table. Write the Selected Metric SWITCH measure. Add the slicer and a chart using Selected Metric. Test switching between metrics.
•  Write the Grid Cost Efficiency measure using VAR/RETURN. Debug by temporarily changing RETURN to each intermediate variable.
•  Organize all measures into display folders: Consumption, Cost, Time Intelligence, Comparison, Ranking.

In Module BI4, we turn these measures into visuals — report design principles (the 5-second rule, visual hierarchy), core visual types for energy dashboards, slicers and interactivity, drill-through for substation detail pages, conditional formatting, and mobile layout. Your DAX measures become stories that drive grid operations decisions.

content: Card visual, slicer set to Region = NORTH
Region = NORTH — only NORTH rows participate.
One evaluation per region. Each row gets its own filter context.
Matrix visual, Region on rows + Year on columns
Table visual inside a drill-through page for SUB-101
Removes all filters from the specified table or column.
ALL(dim_substations[region]) — ignore region slicer for grand total.
Removes all filters EXCEPT the specified columns.
ALLEXCEPT(dim_substations, dim_substations[region]) — keep region, ignore everything else.
Same as ALL but clearer naming (newer function).
Adds a filter that intersects (not replaces) existing filters.
Narrow an existing filter rather than overriding it.
Activates an inactive relationship for this calculation.
USERELATIONSHIP(fact[load_date_key], dim_date[date_key]) — analyze by load date.
YTD grid consumption for progress tracking
MTD consumption for billing period monitoring
Compare this March to last March
Date range ending at a point
Full previous year (all 12 months, not partial)
No row-level calculation needed. Simple aggregation.
Row-level calculation (qty * price) needed before summing.
Average of pre-computed ratios per row. AVERAGE(col1/col2) does not work in DAX.
VAR Cur=[Total Consumption kWh] VAR LY=[Consumption LY] RETURN DIVIDE(Cur-LY, LY)
CALCULATE([Total Consumption kWh], DATESINPERIOD(dim_date[calendar_date], MAX(...), -12, MONTH))
VAR Cur=[Total Consumption kWh] VAR Prior=CALCULATE([Total Consumption kWh], DATEADD(dim_date[calendar_date],-1,MONTH)) RETURN DIVIDE(Cur-Prior,Prior)
DIVIDE([Total Consumption kWh], CALCULATE([Total Consumption kWh], ALL(dim_substations[region])))
DIVIDE([Total Consumption kWh], CALCULATE([Total Consumption kWh], ALLEXCEPT(dim_substations, dim_substations[region])))
DIVIDE(CALCULATE([Total Consumption kWh], TOPN(10, ALL(dim_substations), [Total Consumption kWh], DESC)), [Total Consumption kWh])
VAR Total = [Total Consumption kWh] in complex formulas.
Handles division by zero. Returns 0 or alternate value.
Avoid iterators on large tables when simple aggregation works
SUMX evaluates per row. SUM is vectorized and faster.
Use REMOVEFILTERS instead of ALL when intent is filter removal
Pre-aggregate in the Gold layer when possible
DAX on 500M fact rows is slower than DAX on 12 monthly aggregate rows.
v_monthly_grid_summary as a source table for executive dashboards.
Calculated columns add memory per row. Source columns are free.
Add consumption_band in the engineering pipeline, not in DAX.

chapters.title: Reports & Dashboards
chapters.description: Creating interactive reports and dashboards to deliver insights

blocks.content: Module BI4
Reports, Dashboards & Visualization Design
Turning DAX Measures Into Decisions
Estimated Time: 120 Minutes

Module Objective: Design effective Power BI reports for energy grid analytics — apply the 5-second rule and visual hierarchy, choose the right chart for each insight, build interactive pages with slicers and cross-filtering, create drill-through for substation detail, use conditional formatting for exception highlighting, configure bookmarks for view toggling, leverage AI visuals for self-service exploration, design mobile layouts, and understand when to use paginated reports.
Welcome to Module BI4
Module BI3 built the DAX measure library: 18 measures across consumption, cost, time intelligence, comparison, and ranking. The measures are the logic. Now you make them visible.

Reporting is where the entire platform delivers value to the business. Every Lakehouse, Pipeline, Notebook, Warehouse, stored procedure, and DAX measure exists so that a grid operations manager can open a report and make a better decision. If the report is confusing, cluttered, or misleading, all that upstream work is wasted.

Good reporting is not about making things pretty. It is about clarity, speed, and trust. A well-designed report answers the user’s question in under 5 seconds. The visual hierarchy guides the eye to the most important number first. The interactivity lets the user explore without getting lost. This module teaches the design principles and techniques that make this happen.
Part 1: Report Design Principles
The 5-Second Rule

A report page should answer the primary question within 5 seconds of opening. If a user has to hunt for the key number, the design has failed. Put the most important metric at the top-left — that is where the eye goes first. Use large card visuals or KPIs for headline numbers.
Visual Hierarchy
Level 1 — Headline metrics: Large card or KPI visuals at the top. Total Consumption kWh, YoY Growth %, Active Meters, Peak Demand kW. The answer to “how is the grid performing?”
Level 2 — Trend and comparison: Line charts for time trends (monthly consumption), bar charts for category comparisons (consumption by region). The answer to “what’s changing?”
Level 3 — Detail and exploration: Tables, matrices, and drill-through pages. The answer to “why?” Available on demand, not on the front page.
Energy Grid Report Layout
│ [Slicer: Region] [Slicer: Date Range] [Slicer: VoltageClass] │
│ CARD        │ CARD        │ CARD        │ KPI            │
│ Total kWh   │ Total Cost  │ Active      │ YoY Growth %   │
│ LINE CHART: Monthly Consumption Trend  │ BAR CHART: Consumption  │
│ (Calendar hierarchy on axis,           │ by Region               │
│  Total Consumption kWh + Consumption LY)│ (NORTH, SOUTH, EAST,   │
│                                         │  WEST)                  │
│ TABLE: Top Substations by Consumption (with Substation Rank,   │
│ region, voltage_class, Total kWh, Peak kW, % of Region Total)  │
│ Right-click any row → Drill through to Substation Detail page  │
Color and Formatting
•  Use a consistent color palette: 5–7 distinct colors aligned with your organization’s brand.
•  Use color meaningfully: green for positive YoY growth, red for negative. Do not use color for decoration.
•  Avoid 3D charts, gradients, and unnecessary visual effects. They distort perception.
•  Consistent font sizes: 24pt+ for headlines (card values), 14–18pt for visual titles, 10–12pt for detail labels.
Accessibility
•  Sufficient color contrast (WCAG AA: 4.5:1 ratio for text).
•  Alt text on all visuals for screen reader compatibility.
•  Do not rely on color alone — add labels or patterns.
•  Test with the Accessibility Checker in Power BI Desktop.
Part 2: Visual Selection Guide for Energy Reports
Part 3: Slicers, Filters, and Interactivity
Energy Report Slicers
Cross-Filtering and Edit Interactions
When a user clicks the NORTH bar in the regional consumption chart, every other visual on the page should respond. Configure interactions:
•  Line chart (monthly trend): cross-filter to show only NORTH trend.
•  KPI cards: cross-filter to show NORTH totals.
•  Substation table: cross-filter to show only NORTH substations.

To configure: select the source visual (regional bar chart), then go to Format → Edit Interactions. For each target visual, choose Filter (show only matching), Highlight (dim non-matching), or None (ignore).
Sync Slicers Across Pages

The Region and Date Range slicers should be consistent across all report pages. Use Sync Slicers: select a slicer, then View → Sync Slicers. Check the boxes for which pages the slicer syncs to. When the user changes the region on the overview page, the detail and executive pages update automatically.
Part 4: Drill-Through for Substation Detail
Drill-through lets users navigate from a summary page to a detail page filtered to the selected context. This is the most important navigation pattern for the energy grid report.
Setting Up Substation Drill-Through
•  Create a new page: “Substation Detail.”
•  In the page’s Drill Through section, add dim_substations[substation_name] as the drill-through field.

•  Design the detail page: card visuals for the substation’s Total kWh, Peak kW, Active Meters, Age Years. A line chart showing the substation’s daily consumption trend. A table of individual meter readings at this substation.
•  On the summary page, users right-click any substation name → “Drill through → Substation Detail.” The detail page opens, automatically filtered to that substation.
•  A Back button is added automatically for navigation.
Energy Report Page Structure
Part 5: Conditional Formatting for Exception Highlighting
Conditional formatting makes exceptions visible. Instead of scanning a 50-row table to find problems, the problems jump out.
Energy Conditional Formatting Patterns

Important for energy reports: In energy, consumption growth is typically a concern (higher costs, higher grid stress), not a celebration. Reverse the usual green/red convention: red for increasing consumption, green for decreasing. Make this explicit in the report’s legend or tooltip to avoid confusion.
Part 6: Bookmarks, Buttons, and Navigation
Toggle Views with Bookmarks
The Grid Overview page has both a chart view and a table view. Use bookmarks to let users toggle between them:
•  Create “Chart View” bookmark: line chart and bar chart visible, summary table hidden.
•  Create “Table View” bookmark: line chart and bar chart hidden, full detail table visible.
•  Add two buttons (“Chart View” / “Table View”) that trigger the respective bookmarks.
•  Users click the button to switch. The same data, different presentation.
Reset Filters Button
•  Create a “Reset” bookmark that captures the default slicer state (all regions, last 30 days, all voltage classes).
•  Add a “Reset Filters” button that triggers this bookmark.
•  Users who have drilled into NORTH + Transmission can click Reset to return to the default view.
Page Navigation Buttons

Instead of relying on Power BI’s tab bar, create a left-side navigation panel with buttons for each page: Overview, Regional, Executive, Anomalies. Highlight the current page’s button. This creates a professional, app-like navigation experience.
Part 7: AI-Powered Visuals
Decomposition Tree

Place on the Regional Analysis page. Metric: Total Consumption kWh. Explanation levels: region, voltage_class, substation_name, meter_type. Users click through levels to find what drives consumption in each region. The AI-assisted split automatically recommends the most impactful next level.
Key Influencers

Place on the Anomalies page. Analyze: “What influences consumption_band = High?” Candidate factors: region, voltage_class, meter_type, is_weekend, calendar_month. The visual identifies which factors most strongly correlate with high consumption readings. This helps grid operations prioritize investigations.
Smart Narrative

Place on the Executive Summary page. It auto-generates text: “Total consumption for the selected period was 487.5M kWh, up 6.2% year-over-year. NORTH region accounted for the largest share at 29.2%. Peak demand of 12,300 kW occurred on March 8.” The narrative updates dynamically as users change slicers.
Q&A Visual

Add to the Grid Overview page. Users type natural language questions: “Total consumption by region this quarter” → bar chart. “Top 10 substations by peak demand” → table. Clean column names in the semantic model (BI2) directly improve Q&A accuracy.
Part 8: Mobile Layout
Field crews and operations managers access dashboards on mobile devices. Power BI’s mobile layout lets you create a phone-optimized view of each report page.
•  In Power BI Desktop: View → Mobile Layout. A phone-shaped canvas appears.
•  Drag existing visuals from the desktop canvas onto the mobile canvas. Resize and reposition for a vertical layout.
•  Prioritize: KPI cards at the top (Total kWh, Peak Demand, Active Meters), one chart below (consumption by region or daily trend), slicers accessible via the filter pane.
•  Remove detail tables from mobile — they are unreadable on small screens. Add a “View Full Report” button that opens the desktop layout.
•  Test in the Power BI mobile app. Verify that slicer interactions and drill-through work on touch devices.
Part 9: Paginated Reports — When to Use Them

Standard Power BI reports are designed for interactive screen exploration. Paginated reports are designed for pixel-perfect, print-ready output. Most energy dashboards use standard reports. Paginated reports serve specific use cases:

Paginated reports are authored in Power BI Report Builder or the Fabric web editor. They connect to the same semantic model or directly to the Warehouse SQL endpoint. For the energy capstone, you will primarily build standard interactive reports; paginated reports are an optional extension for billing and regulatory use cases.
Module BI4 Checkpoint
You now understand:

•  Design principles — the 5-second rule (top-left headline), 3-level visual hierarchy (headline → trend → detail), color and formatting conventions (meaningful color, 5–7 palette, reverse green/red for energy), accessibility standards

•  Visual selection — 13-visual reference table with energy examples and “avoid when” guidance; cards for headlines, lines for trends, bars for comparisons, tables for detail, scatter for correlation, decomposition tree for AI-driven breakdown
•  Slicers and interactivity — 5 energy slicers (Region, Date Range, Voltage Class, Meter Type, Consumption Band), cross-filtering configuration (Filter/Highlight/None), sync slicers across pages
•  Drill-through — substation detail page with drill-through field, automatic Back button, 5-page report structure (Overview, Regional Analysis, Substation Detail, Executive Summary, Anomalies)
•  Conditional formatting — 5 patterns (background color for overload, reversed green/red for energy growth, icon sets for quality flags, data bars for magnitude, font color for ranking)
•  Bookmarks and navigation — chart/table view toggle, reset filters button, left-side page navigation panel
•  AI visuals — Decomposition Tree (what drives consumption?), Key Influencers (what correlates with high readings?), Smart Narrative (auto-generated executive text), Q&A (natural language querying)
•  Mobile layout — phone canvas with KPI cards at top, one chart, no detail tables, “View Full Report” button
•  Paginated reports — when to use (billing statements, regulatory compliance, multi-page exports, print output); authored in Report Builder; same semantic model connection
Hands-On Lab

•  Create a new report connected to sm_grid_analytics. Build the Grid Overview page following the layout diagram: 4 KPI cards at top (Total kWh, Total Cost EUR, Active Meters, YoY Growth %), monthly trend line chart with prior year overlay, regional consumption bar chart, top substations table with Substation Rank.
•  Add slicers: Region (list), Date Range (relative date, default Last 30 days), Voltage Class (dropdown). Configure slicer sync across pages.
•  Configure cross-filtering: click a region bar and verify all other visuals filter to that region. Set Edit Interactions to Filter mode for cards and trend chart.

•  Create a Substation Detail drill-through page with substation_name as the drill-through field. Add substation KPIs, daily consumption trend, and meter readings table. Test: right-click a substation → Drill through.

•  Apply conditional formatting: red background on the kWh column for substations above 3x regional average. Red font on YoY Growth % when positive (energy growth = concern). Green checkmark icon on quality_flag = VALID.
•  Create Chart View and Table View bookmarks for the Overview page. Add toggle buttons.
•  Add an AI visual: Decomposition Tree on the Regional Analysis page with Total Consumption kWh and explanation levels (region, voltage_class, substation_name).
•  Create a mobile layout for the Overview page: 3 KPI cards at top, one regional bar chart below.
•  Test the complete report: navigate between pages, use slicers, drill through to substation detail, toggle views, and verify all measures respond correctly.

In Module BI5, we distribute this report to the business — Power BI Apps for governed packaging, workspace deployment, subscriptions for scheduled delivery, embedding in Teams and SharePoint, and paginated report integration. Your report is built. Now it needs to reach the right people at the right time.
