chapters.title: Distribution & Apps
chapters.description: Distributing content and building Power Apps for enterprise BI solutions

blocks.content: Module BI5
Distribution, Apps & Subscriptions
Getting Reports to the Right People at the Right Time
Estimated Time: 90 Minutes

Module Objective: Distribute energy grid reports to the business — Power BI Apps for governed packaging, dashboards for executive monitoring, email subscriptions for scheduled delivery, embedding in Teams and SharePoint, Copilot for AI-assisted exploration, and the sharing method decision framework.
Welcome to Module BI5

Module BI4 built the report: 5 pages, 18 DAX measures visualized, slicers, drill-through, conditional formatting, bookmarks. The report is ready. But it sits in your workspace — a development environment. No business user sees it until you distribute it.

Distribution is the last mile of business intelligence. A brilliant report that nobody opens has zero business impact. The grid operations manager needs it in their morning Teams channel. The CFO needs a monthly PDF in their inbox. The field crew needs it on their phone. Copilot lets any user ask questions about the data in natural language. This module covers every distribution channel and the governance principles that keep them secure.

This module covers: Power BI Apps for governed packaging, dashboards for pinned KPI monitoring, email subscriptions for scheduled delivery, embedding in Teams and SharePoint, the sharing method decision framework, Copilot in Power BI for AI-assisted exploration, and workspace-to-App deployment workflow.
Part 1: Power BI Apps — The Production Distribution Mechanism

Rule: always distribute through Apps, not through direct workspace access or individual report links. Apps provide governance (controlled release), scalability (audience management via security groups), and professionalism (branded navigation).
Why Apps, Not Direct Workspace Access

Separation of concerns — The workspace is your development environment. It contains draft reports, staging datasets, and work-in-progress. The App is the production artifact. Users see only the finished, approved reports.
Audience management — Apps have their own permissions, independent of workspace roles. You grant App access to a broad audience without giving them workspace Contributor or Member access.
Update control — When you update a report in the workspace, the App is not affected until you explicitly publish a new App version. No accidental exposure of half-finished changes.
Branded navigation — Apps provide a left navigation pane with organized sections. Users navigate between reports in a structured, intuitive layout that you design.
Creating the NordGrid Energy App
•  Navigate to ws_nordgrid_bi workspace. Click “Create app.”
•  Configure: Name = “NordGrid Grid Analytics.” Description = “Daily grid consumption, regional analysis, and substation detail.” Add the NordGrid logo.
•  Select content: Grid Overview, Regional Analysis, Executive Summary, Meter Anomalies pages. Exclude internal draft pages.
•  Configure navigation: Section 1 = “Operational” (Overview, Regional, Anomalies). Section 2 = “Executive” (Executive Summary).
•  Configure audience: GridOperationsTeam security group, ExecutiveTeam security group. Or specific users by email.
•  Publish. Users find the App in the Power BI App Marketplace or receive a direct install link.
App Update Workflow
•  Make changes to reports in the workspace (edit visuals, add a new DAX measure, fix a formatting issue).
•  Test changes by viewing reports directly in the workspace.
•  When ready, click “Update app.” The new version is published immediately.
•  Users see the updated content the next time they open the App. No reinstall needed.
Part 2: Dashboards — Pinned KPI Monitoring
A dashboard is a single canvas of pinned tiles from one or more reports. Dashboards are curated executive views — the answer to “how are we doing?” at a glance.
Dashboards vs. Reports
NordGrid Executive Dashboard
Pin tiles from the Grid Overview report:
•  Pin: Total Consumption kWh card → dashboard tile (top-left).
•  Pin: YoY Growth % KPI card → dashboard tile.
•  Pin: Peak Demand kW card → dashboard tile.
•  Pin: Monthly consumption trend line → dashboard tile (spans full width below cards).
•  Pin: Regional consumption bar chart from the Regional Analysis report → same dashboard.
The dashboard now shows tiles from two report pages on a single screen. Clicking any tile navigates to the source report for deeper analysis.
Real-Time Streaming Tiles

For operational monitoring, dashboards support streaming tiles that update without manual refresh. Connect a streaming dataset from an Eventstream (Real-Time Intelligence track) to display live grid metrics: current demand, active alerts, and SCADA sensor status. Standard pinned tiles from DirectLake reports update on each dashboard load; streaming tiles update in sub-second intervals.
Part 3: Email Subscriptions for Scheduled Delivery

Email subscriptions deliver report snapshots to users’ inboxes on a schedule. The CFO receives the Executive Summary as a PDF every Monday morning. The grid operations manager receives the Anomalies page daily at 8:00 AM.
Configuring a Subscription
•  Open a report page in the Fabric portal.
•  Click Subscribe → “Subscribe to this page.”
•  Set schedule: daily, weekly (specific days), or after data refresh.
•  Set time: 08:00 AM UTC (align with pipeline completion — the pipeline refreshes Gold at 02:30 AM, so data is ready by morning).
•  Add recipients: the subscriber’s own email, or additional users/groups.
•  Set format: image snapshot (default), PDF attachment, or PowerPoint attachment.
•  Set subject line: “NordGrid Daily Grid Report — {date}.”
Subscription Security

Important: Subscriptions respect Row-Level Security. If the NORTH region manager subscribes, the email snapshot contains only NORTH data. Each subscriber sees their own data, even in the same subscription. This is critical for the energy platform where regional managers must not see other regions’ data.
Energy Subscription Plan
Part 4: Embedding in Teams and SharePoint
Embedding in Microsoft Teams
Grid operations teams live in Teams. Embedding the report directly in a Teams channel eliminates the need to switch to the Fabric portal:
•  Open the Teams channel (e.g., #grid-operations).
•  Click “+” to add a tab → select “Power BI.”
•  Select the Grid Overview report or the NordGrid App.
•  The report renders inline in the Teams tab. Full interactivity: slicers, drill-through, bookmarks all work.
•  RLS is enforced based on the Teams user’s identity. The NORTH manager sees only NORTH data even in Teams.
•  Conversations in the Teams channel can reference specific data points from the embedded report.
Embedding in SharePoint
For organizations using SharePoint as their intranet, embed reports on SharePoint pages:
•  Edit the SharePoint page. Add the “Power BI” web part.
•  Paste the report URL or select from available reports.
•  Configure the web part: show/hide filter pane, navigation, action bar.
•  RLS is enforced based on the SharePoint user’s identity.
Part 5: Sharing Method Decision Framework

Warning: Publish to web creates a completely public URL with zero authentication. Anyone with the link sees the data. There is no RLS, no login, no audit trail. Never use Publish to web for NordGrid energy data or any confidential/internal data. This is the most common Power BI security mistake.
Part 6: Copilot in Power BI
Copilot brings generative AI into the Power BI experience. It accelerates report creation, DAX authoring, and data exploration.
What Copilot Can Do

Generate report pages — Describe what you want (“Create a page showing monthly consumption trend by region with a year-over-year comparison”) and Copilot builds the page with visuals, measures, and formatting.
Write DAX measures — Describe the calculation (“Create a measure for rolling 12-month average consumption per meter”) and Copilot writes the DAX formula.
Create narrative summaries — Copilot generates a Smart Narrative describing trends, outliers, and key figures in natural language.
Answer questions — Users ask: “Which region had the highest consumption growth this quarter?” Copilot responds with a natural language answer and supporting visual.
Using Copilot Effectively

•  Be specific in prompts. “Create a consumption report” is too vague. “Create a page with monthly kWh trend as a line chart, regional bar chart below, and 4 KPI cards for Total kWh, Peak kW, Active Meters, YoY Growth” is actionable.
•  Review generated DAX. Copilot writes correct DAX most of the time, but always verify against your business rules. Check edge cases (division by zero, null handling, filter context).
•  Iterate. Copilot’s first output is a starting point. Refine visuals, adjust formatting, and modify measures to match your standards.
•  Use Copilot for acceleration, not replacement. It handles boilerplate so you can focus on nuance.
Copilot Requirements
•  Fabric F64 or higher capacity (or Power BI Premium per capacity).
•  Enabled by the Fabric admin in the admin portal.
•  Semantic model must be in a workspace on an eligible capacity.
Copilot capabilities evolve continuously. Check the Fabric release notes for the latest features.
Part 7: Workspace-to-App Deployment Workflow
For enterprise-grade distribution, separate development from production using the workspace-to-App pattern:
ws_nordgrid_bi_dev (Development Workspace)
│  └── Draft reports, experimental measures, testing
▼  Promote to production when ready
ws_nordgrid_bi (Production Workspace)
│  └── Approved reports, validated measures
▼  Publish App
NordGrid Grid Analytics (Power BI App)
└── Users see only this — branded, governed, versioned

For organizations with Fabric deployment pipelines (covered in the Administration track), this can be automated: Dev workspace → Test workspace → Prod workspace, with the App published from Prod only. The development, testing, and production stages are separate, and the App is the only interface visible to business users.
Module BI5 Checkpoint
You now understand:

•  Power BI Apps — governed distribution: workspace is development, App is production; audience management via security groups; update workflow (edit → test → publish); branded navigation with sections

•  Dashboards — single-page pinned tiles from multiple reports; dashboards vs. reports (5-point comparison); creating the NordGrid executive dashboard; real-time streaming tiles for operational monitoring
•  Email subscriptions — scheduled delivery of report snapshots; configuration (schedule, format, recipients); RLS per subscriber; 6-recipient energy subscription plan table
•  Embedding — Teams tab for grid operations channel (full interactivity, RLS enforced); SharePoint web part for intranet; both respect user identity

•  Sharing decision framework — 9-method comparison table (App, Dashboard, Teams, SharePoint, subscription, direct link, publish to web, Embed API, PDF export) with audience, security, interactivity, and energy use case
•  Copilot — AI-assisted report generation, DAX writing, narrative summaries, natural language Q&A; effective prompting; review and iterate; capacity requirements
•  Workspace-to-App deployment — Dev workspace → Prod workspace → App; separation of development from production; deployment pipeline integration
Hands-On Lab

•  Create a Power BI App from your ws_nordgrid_bi workspace. Name it “NordGrid Grid Analytics.” Select the Grid Overview, Regional Analysis, and Executive Summary pages. Configure two navigation sections: “Operational” and “Executive.” Publish to a test audience.

•  Create a dashboard. Pin 3 KPI cards (Total kWh, YoY Growth %, Peak Demand kW) and the monthly trend line chart from the Grid Overview report. Pin the regional bar chart from the Regional Analysis report. Verify that clicking a tile navigates to the source report.

•  Create an email subscription: subscribe yourself to the Grid Overview page. Set schedule to daily. Set format to PDF. Verify delivery (you may need to wait for the next scheduled run or trigger manually).
•  Embed the Grid Overview report in a Teams channel tab. Verify full interactivity: slicers, cross-filtering, and drill-through work within Teams.

•  If Copilot is available on your capacity, test: ask Copilot to generate a DAX measure for “average consumption per meter for the NORTH region only.” Review the generated formula. Verify it matches your expected result.
•  Update the App: add a new measure to the report in the workspace, then click “Update app.” Open the App as a user and verify the new measure appears.

In Module BI6, we lock it down with Row-Level Security & Governance — DAX-based RLS in the semantic model, Object-Level Security for hiding sensitive columns, dynamic RLS with security mapping tables, sensitivity labels, endorsement (Certified/Promoted), lineage tracking, and the complete security architecture for energy grid reporting.

content: Pinned tiles from one or more reports.
Full visuals created in the designer.
Click a tile to navigate to the source report.
Can combine tiles from different semantic models.
Primary distribution for all grid analytics reports.
Full RLS. Workspace or App permissions.
Full RLS. SSO via Entra ID.
Grid operations team channel. Daily monitoring.
Full RLS. SharePoint permissions + Power BI access.
Monday morning executive PDF. Daily anomaly alerts.
NEVER for NordGrid energy data. Only for public datasets.
Customer-facing energy portal for large B2B customers.
Board meeting presentation. Regulatory submission attachment.

chapters.title: Row-Level Security & Governance
chapters.description: Implementing Row-Level Security and governance policies for secure BI

blocks.content: Module BI6
Row-Level Security & Governance for BI
Every User Sees Only Their Data
Estimated Time: 90 Minutes

Module Objective: Secure the energy grid semantic model with Row-Level Security (static and dynamic), Object-Level Security for hiding sensitive columns, implement the security mapping table pattern for scalable regional access, test RLS with “View as role,” understand the gap between semantic model security and source-level security, apply sensitivity labels and endorsement, use lineage and impact analysis for governance, and build the complete BI security architecture.
Welcome to Module BI6

Module BI5 distributed the report to the business: Apps, dashboards, subscriptions, Teams embedding. But distribution without security is a governance failure. The NORTH region manager must not see SOUTH region data. The billing team must not see customer PII. The executive summary must show all regions but only to authorized executives.

Row-Level Security (RLS) is the mechanism that makes this work. RLS in the semantic model applies DAX filter rules that automatically restrict which rows a user can see based on their identity. It is transparent — the user’s report looks normal, but every measure evaluates only against their authorized data. The NORTH manager’s Total Consumption kWh shows only NORTH consumption, even though the same measure formula serves all regions.

This module covers: static RLS for simple role-based access, dynamic RLS with USERPRINCIPALNAME() and security mapping tables, Object-Level Security for hiding sensitive columns, testing RLS, the semantic model vs. source-level security gap, sensitivity labels, endorsement (Certified/Promoted), lineage and impact analysis, and the complete BI security architecture for the energy platform.
Part 1: Static RLS — Simple Role-Based Access
Static RLS defines fixed filter rules per role. Each role has a hard-coded DAX filter expression that restricts the data visible to members of that role.
Creating Static Roles for Energy Regions
In the semantic model editor (web modeler or Power BI Desktop), navigate to Security → Manage roles:
// Role: NORTH Region Manager
// Filter on dim_substations table:
[region] = "NORTH"
// Role: SOUTH Region Manager
// Filter on dim_substations table:
[region] = "SOUTH"
// Role: EAST Region Manager
[region] = "EAST"
// Role: WEST Region Manager
[region] = "WEST"
How Static RLS Works

When the NORTH Region Manager opens a report, DAX automatically adds the filter [region] = “NORTH” to every query. The filter is applied on dim_substations, which then cascades through the star schema relationship to fact_meter_readings. Every measure evaluates only against NORTH data:
•  Total Consumption kWh shows NORTH-only consumption.
•  The region slicer shows only NORTH (other values are filtered out).
•  The substation table shows only NORTH substations.
•  YoY Growth % calculates growth for NORTH only.
The filter is transparent — the user cannot override it. Even if they clear all slicers, the RLS filter persists.
Assigning Users to Roles
•  In the Fabric portal, open the semantic model’s security settings.
•  For each role, add users or Entra ID security groups.
•  Example: add the “NordGrid-NORTH-Managers” security group to the “NORTH Region Manager” role.
•  Users in multiple roles see the union of all roles’ data (e.g., a user in both NORTH and SOUTH roles sees both regions).
Limitation of Static RLS

Static RLS requires one role per filter value. With 4 regions, you need 4 roles. Manageable. But if you need per-user access (100 analysts, each seeing a different combination of substations), static RLS does not scale. This is where dynamic RLS comes in.
Part 2: Dynamic RLS — Scalable User-Based Access

Dynamic RLS uses a single role with a DAX filter that evaluates the logged-in user’s identity at query time. Instead of hard-coding [region] = “NORTH” per role, you create a security mapping table that maps each user’s email to their authorized regions. One role serves all users.
Step 1: Create the Security Mapping Table
-- In the Warehouse (gold schema) or as a table in the Lakehouse
CREATE TABLE gold.rls_user_region_mapping (
user_email   VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
region       VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL
INSERT INTO gold.rls_user_region_mapping VALUES
('anna.north@nordgrid.com', 'NORTH'),
('erik.south@nordgrid.com', 'SOUTH'),
('maria.ops@nordgrid.com', 'NORTH'),
('maria.ops@nordgrid.com', 'SOUTH'),  -- Maria sees NORTH + SOUTH
('maria.ops@nordgrid.com', 'EAST'),
('maria.ops@nordgrid.com', 'WEST'),   -- Maria sees all 4 regions
('cfo@nordgrid.com', 'NORTH'),
('cfo@nordgrid.com', 'SOUTH'),
('cfo@nordgrid.com', 'EAST'),
('cfo@nordgrid.com', 'WEST');          -- CFO sees everything
Step 2: Add the Table to the Semantic Model
•  Include rls_user_region_mapping in the semantic model (it comes from the Gold Lakehouse or Warehouse via DirectLake).

•  Create a relationship: rls_user_region_mapping[region] (many) → dim_substations[region] (one). Set cross-filter direction to Both (this is the one exception where bi-directional is required — the security table must filter the dimension which then filters the fact).
•  Hide the rls_user_region_mapping table from the Fields pane — it is a security table, not for report building.
Step 3: Create the Dynamic RLS Role
// Single role: Regional Access
// Filter on rls_user_region_mapping table:
[user_email] = USERPRINCIPALNAME()

USERPRINCIPALNAME() returns the logged-in user’s Entra ID email address at query time. When anna.north@nordgrid.com opens the report, DAX filters rls_user_region_mapping to her row (region = NORTH). This filter cascades through the bi-directional relationship to dim_substations, then through the one-to-many relationship to fact_meter_readings. Anna sees only NORTH data.

When maria.ops@nordgrid.com opens the report, DAX filters rls_user_region_mapping to her 4 rows (all regions). She sees all data. One role, different data per user, driven entirely by the mapping table.
Dynamic RLS Flow Diagram
User opens report (anna.north@nordgrid.com)
USERPRINCIPALNAME() = 'anna.north@nordgrid.com'
rls_user_region_mapping filtered to: region = 'NORTH'
│  (bi-directional relationship)
dim_substations filtered to: region = 'NORTH'
│  (one-to-many relationship)
fact_meter_readings filtered to: NORTH substations only
All DAX measures evaluate on NORTH data only
Total Consumption kWh = 142,200,000 (NORTH only)
Advantages of Dynamic RLS Over Static

Best practice for production: Always use dynamic RLS with a security mapping table. Static RLS is acceptable only for quick prototyping or very simple scenarios (2–3 fixed roles). The mapping table approach is manageable (the engineering team maintains the table), auditable (query the table to see who has access to what), and scalable.
Part 3: Object-Level Security — Hiding Sensitive Columns
Object-Level Security (OLS) hides entire tables or columns from specific roles. While RLS controls which rows a user sees, OLS controls which columns or tables are visible.
Energy OLS Example
The dim_meters table contains customer_name and install_address columns. The grid operations team needs meter data for load analysis but must not see customer PII:
// OLS configuration (via XMLA endpoint or Tabular Editor):
// Role: Grid Operations
//   dim_meters[customer_name] = None  (hidden)
//   dim_meters[install_address] = None (hidden)
//   All other columns = Read (visible)

When a grid operations analyst opens the report, customer_name and install_address do not appear in the Fields pane. Any visual referencing these columns shows an error for this role. The analyst can still see meter_id, meter_type, tariff_code, and all consumption measures.
OLS Configuration Tools
•  Tabular Editor — the most common tool for configuring OLS. Connect to the semantic model, navigate to roles, and set column-level permissions (Read, None).
•  XMLA endpoint — for scripting OLS configuration via TMSL (Tabular Model Scripting Language).
•  The Fabric web modeler does not support OLS configuration directly (as of current release). Use Tabular Editor or XMLA.
RLS + OLS Together
RLS and OLS complement each other:
•  RLS (rows): NORTH manager sees only NORTH data.
•  OLS (columns): Grid operations team cannot see customer PII columns.
•  A user in both the NORTH role (RLS) and the Grid Operations role (OLS) sees only NORTH data with PII columns hidden.
Part 4: Testing RLS — “View as Role”
Testing is non-negotiable. You must verify that every role sees exactly the correct data before publishing the App.
Testing in the Web Modeler
•  Open the semantic model in the Fabric portal.
•  Navigate to Security → select a role → “Test as role.”
•  The report renders with that role’s RLS filters applied.
•  For dynamic RLS: enter a test email address (e.g., anna.north@nordgrid.com) to simulate USERPRINCIPALNAME().
Testing Checklist for Energy RLS

Warning: RLS in the semantic model applies to Power BI reports, Apps, subscriptions, and embedded content. It does NOT restrict access to the underlying Lakehouse or Warehouse tables. A user with direct SQL access to the Warehouse bypasses semantic model RLS entirely. For complete protection, enforce security at both layers: Warehouse-level RLS (DW2 Part 8) AND semantic model RLS.
Part 5: Sensitivity Labels and Information Protection
Sensitivity labels classify data by confidentiality level. They are applied to Fabric items (semantic models, reports, dashboards) and control what users can do with the data.
Sensitivity Label Hierarchy
•  Labels are configured by the Fabric admin via Microsoft Purview (Administration track, Module ADM2).
•  BI developers apply labels to semantic models and reports: open the item’s settings, select the appropriate sensitivity label.
•  Labels inherit downstream: a semantic model labeled “Confidential” automatically applies “Confidential” to any report built on it.
•  Labels enforce restrictions: a “Confidential” report cannot be published to web. A “Highly Confidential” dataset cannot be exported to Excel.
Part 6: Endorsement — Certified and Promoted
Endorsement marks semantic models and reports as trusted, making them discoverable and distinguishing production content from experimental work.
Endorsement for the Energy Platform
•  Certify sm_grid_analytics as the official energy grid semantic model. All reports must connect to this model.
•  Promote report versions that have passed testing but are awaiting formal certification.
•  Unendorsed models are visible but not recommended — users see a visual distinction in the Fabric portal.
Endorsement prevents the “50 semantic models and nobody knows which one is correct” problem. When a user searches for energy data, the Certified model appears first with a badge.
Part 7: Lineage and Impact Analysis
Lineage shows how data flows from source to report. Impact analysis shows what downstream items are affected when you change something upstream.
Energy Platform Lineage
lh_bronze_grid (Lakehouse)       ← Source files land here
wh_grid_analytics (Warehouse)    ← staging → silver → gold
sm_grid_analytics (Semantic Model) ← DirectLake on Gold tables
├── rpt_grid_overview (Report)    ← Grid Overview + Regional + Detail
├── rpt_executive_summary (Report)
├── dash_executive (Dashboard)    ← Pinned tiles from reports
└── NordGrid Grid Analytics (App)  ← Published from workspace
View lineage in the Fabric portal: open the workspace, click “Lineage view.” The visual graph shows every item and its dependencies.
Impact Analysis
Before modifying the Gold star schema (e.g., renaming a column, dropping a table), check the impact:
•  Open the Warehouse or Lakehouse in the Fabric portal.
•  Select a table (e.g., fact_meter_readings) → “View impact analysis.”
•  The portal shows which semantic models, reports, and dashboards depend on this table.
•  If 3 reports and 1 App depend on fact_meter_readings, renaming a column will break them unless you update the semantic model first.
Impact analysis prevents accidental breakage. Always check before modifying Gold-layer tables.
Part 8: The Complete BI Security Architecture

Defense in depth: No single layer is sufficient. Warehouse RLS protects SQL access. Semantic model RLS protects Power BI access. OLS hides sensitive columns. Sensitivity labels prevent unauthorized export. Endorsement prevents users from connecting to the wrong model. Together, they form a complete security envelope around NordGrid’s energy data.
Module BI6 Checkpoint
You now understand:
•  Static RLS — hard-coded DAX filter per role ([region] = "NORTH"); one role per filter value; assigning users to roles; limitation at scale (too many roles)

•  Dynamic RLS — security mapping table (rls_user_region_mapping) with user_email + region; USERPRINCIPALNAME() in a single DAX role; bi-directional relationship from mapping table to dimension; flow diagram from user login through filter cascading to measure evaluation; 6-point comparison vs. static RLS
•  Object-Level Security — hiding columns (customer_name, install_address) from specific roles; configured via Tabular Editor or XMLA endpoint; RLS + OLS complement each other (rows + columns)
•  Testing RLS — “View as role” in web modeler with simulated email; 6-test checklist (NORTH manager, CFO, no mapping, multi-region, OLS); the semantic model vs. source-level security gap

•  Sensitivity labels — 4-level hierarchy (Public, General, Confidential, Highly Confidential) with restrictions per level; label inheritance from semantic model to report; configured via Microsoft Purview
•  Endorsement — No endorsement (draft), Promoted (team-validated), Certified (organization-approved); prevents the “which model is correct” problem; Certified model appears first in search
•  Lineage and impact analysis — lineage view showing source → Warehouse → semantic model → reports → App; impact analysis before modifying Gold tables; prevents accidental breakage

•  Complete BI security architecture — 9-layer table from platform (Entra ID) through workspace, item, App, rows (RLS), columns (OLS), source (Warehouse RLS), classification (labels), to trust (endorsement)
Hands-On Lab
•  Create a static RLS role: “NORTH Manager” with filter [region] = "NORTH" on dim_substations. Assign your test account. Test with “View as role” — verify only NORTH data is visible.
•  Create the security mapping table (gold.rls_user_region_mapping) in your Warehouse. Insert mappings for at least 3 users: one single-region, one multi-region, and one all-regions.
•  Add rls_user_region_mapping to the semantic model. Create a bi-directional relationship to dim_substations[region]. Hide the mapping table from the Fields pane.
•  Create a dynamic RLS role: “Regional Access” with filter [user_email] = USERPRINCIPALNAME() on rls_user_region_mapping. Delete the static roles (replace with dynamic).
•  Test dynamic RLS: use “View as role” with each test email. Verify the single-region user sees only their region, the multi-region user sees their regions, and the all-regions user sees everything.
•  Test the edge case: simulate a user email that has no rows in the mapping table. Verify the report shows blank visuals (no data), not an error and not all data.
•  Apply a sensitivity label (“Confidential”) to sm_grid_analytics. Verify that the label inherits to reports built on this model.
•  Endorse sm_grid_analytics as “Certified.” Open the Fabric portal and verify the Certified badge appears. Search for energy data and confirm the Certified model ranks first.

•  Open the lineage view for your workspace. Trace the flow from Lakehouse/Warehouse through the semantic model to reports and the App. Run impact analysis on the fact_meter_readings table and note which downstream items depend on it.

In Module BI7 (Capstone), you build the complete enterprise BI solution end-to-end — from a business brief through semantic model creation, DAX measure library, multi-page interactive report, governed distribution via App, dynamic RLS, and full security architecture. Everything from the BI track comes together in one integrated project.

blocks.content: Module BI7
Capstone — Enterprise BI Solution
Everything You Have Learned, Unified Into One Analytical Experience
Estimated Time: 180 Minutes

Module Objective: Apply everything from Modules F1 through BI6 to build a complete, production-ready enterprise BI solution for NordGrid Energy — DirectLake semantic model with star schema relationships, 20+ DAX measures, multi-page interactive report, governed distribution via Power BI App, dynamic Row-Level Security, sensitivity labels, and endorsement.
Welcome to the Capstone

Seven modules. Seventy lessons. You understand the data layer (BI1), the semantic model engine (BI2), DAX from filter context through time intelligence and ranking (BI3), report design from visual hierarchy through drill-through and AI visuals (BI4), distribution from Apps through subscriptions and Teams embedding (BI5), and security from RLS through sensitivity labels and endorsement (BI6).

Now you build it all. The capstone is a project, not a tutorial. You will receive a business brief, design the semantic model, write the DAX measures, build the report pages, configure security, distribute via App, and verify end-to-end. Every design decision is yours.

There is no step-by-step code in this module. Each phase describes what to build and the acceptance criteria. Choose the patterns, write the DAX, design the visuals, and make the trade-offs. This is how real BI solutions are built.
Phase 1: Business Brief

You are the lead BI developer for NordGrid Energy. The data engineering team has built a Gold-layer star schema (either in a Lakehouse or Warehouse). Your job is to build the complete analytical experience on top of it.
Available Gold-Layer Tables
Business Requirements
Phase 2: Semantic Model — sm_grid_analytics
Deliverables
•  Create a new semantic model (sm_grid_analytics) from the Gold Lakehouse or Warehouse in DirectLake mode.
•  Include tables: fact_meter_readings, dim_date, dim_substations, dim_meters, dim_tariffs, rls_user_region_mapping.

•  Define 4 star schema relationships: dim_date → fact (date_key), dim_substations → fact (substation_sk), dim_meters → fact (meter_sk), dim_tariffs → dim_meters (tariff_code). All one-to-many, single filter direction.
•  Define 1 security relationship: rls_user_region_mapping → dim_substations (region). Bi-directional cross-filter.
•  Create hierarchies: Calendar (year → quarter → month → date), Geography (region → voltage_class → substation_name), Meter Type (meter_type → tariff_code).
•  Hide all surrogate key columns (meter_sk, substation_sk, date_key) and the fact table’s FK columns. Hide rls_user_region_mapping from the Fields pane.
•  Disable auto date/time. Mark dim_date as the date table on calendar_date.
•  Configure display folders: Consumption Measures, Cost Measures, Time Intelligence, Comparison, Ranking, Calendar Attributes, Substation Attributes.
Acceptance Criteria
•  DirectLake mode confirmed in model settings.
•  5 relationships defined (4 star schema + 1 security). No relationship errors.
•  3 hierarchies created. Drill-down works in a test visual.
•  FK columns hidden. Security table hidden. Display folders organized.
•  Auto date/time disabled. dim_date marked as date table.
Modules Referenced: BI1 (data layer evaluation), BI2 (DirectLake model creation, relationships, hierarchies, display folders, optimization).
Phase 3: DAX Measure Library — 20+ Measures
Deliverables — Write and test all measures:
Consumption Measures (5)
•  Total Consumption kWh = SUM(fact_meter_readings[daily_kwh])
•  Peak Demand kW = MAX(fact_meter_readings[peak_kw])
•  Active Meters = DISTINCTCOUNT(fact_meter_readings[meter_sk])
•  Avg Daily kWh = DIVIDE([Total Consumption kWh], [Active Meters], 0)
•  High-Consumption Readings = CALCULATE(COUNTROWS(fact), fact[consumption_band]="High")
Cost Measures (3)
•  Total Cost EUR = SUM(fact_meter_readings[estimated_cost_eur])
•  Avg Cost Per Meter = DIVIDE([Total Cost EUR], [Active Meters], 0)
•  Revenue Per kWh = DIVIDE([Total Cost EUR], [Total Consumption kWh], 0)
Time Intelligence (6)
•  YTD Consumption = TOTALYTD([Total Consumption kWh], dim_date[calendar_date])
•  MTD Consumption = TOTALMTD([Total Consumption kWh], dim_date[calendar_date])
•  Consumption LY = CALCULATE([Total Consumption kWh], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(dim_date[calendar_date]))
•  YoY Growth % = VAR Cur=[Total Consumption kWh] VAR LY=[Consumption LY] RETURN DIVIDE(Cur-LY, LY, 0)
•  Rolling 12M Consumption = CALCULATE([Total Consumption kWh], DATESINPERIOD(dim_date[calendar_date], MAX(dim_date[calendar_date]), -12, MONTH))
•  MoM Growth % = VAR Cur=[Total Consumption kWh] VAR Prior=CALCULATE([Total Consumption kWh], DATEADD(dim_date[calendar_date],-1,MONTH)) RETURN DIVIDE(Cur-Prior, Prior, 0)
Comparison (3)
•  Consumption % of Total = DIVIDE([Total Consumption kWh], CALCULATE([Total Consumption kWh], ALL(dim_substations[region])))
•  Consumption % of Region = DIVIDE([Total Consumption kWh], CALCULATE([Total Consumption kWh], ALLEXCEPT(dim_substations, dim_substations[region])))
•  Cost % of Total = DIVIDE([Total Cost EUR], CALCULATE([Total Cost EUR], ALL(dim_substations[region])))
Ranking (2)
•  Substation Rank = RANKX(ALL(dim_substations), [Total Consumption kWh],,DESC,DENSE)
•  Top 10 Concentration % = DIVIDE(CALCULATE([Total Consumption kWh], TOPN(10, ALL(dim_substations), [Total Consumption kWh], DESC)), [Total Consumption kWh])
Dynamic (1+)

•  Selected Metric = SWITCH(SELECTEDVALUE(MetricSelector[Metric]), "Consumption kWh", [Total Consumption kWh], "Peak Demand kW", [Peak Demand kW], "Cost EUR", [Total Cost EUR], "Active Meters", [Active Meters], BLANK()). Create the disconnected MetricSelector table.
Acceptance Criteria
•  20+ measures created and organized into display folders.
•  All measures use DIVIDE() for division (no / operator).
•  Time intelligence measures work with the Calendar hierarchy (drill from year to month).
•  Comparison measures: Consumption % of Total sums to 100% across all regions in a matrix.
•  Substation Rank = 1 for the highest consumer. Verified in a table visual.
•  Selected Metric responds to the MetricSelector slicer.
Modules Referenced: BI3 (all DAX patterns: CALCULATE, ALL, ALLEXCEPT, time intelligence, SUMX, RANKX, TOPN, SWITCH, VAR/RETURN).
Phase 4: Report Pages — 5 Interactive Pages
Slicers (synced across pages)
•  Region: list slicer on dim_substations[region]. Multi-select. Synced to all pages.
•  Date Range: relative date slicer on dim_date[calendar_date]. Default: Last 30 days. Synced to all pages.
•  Voltage Class: dropdown on dim_substations[voltage_class]. Synced to Overview and Regional pages.
Interactivity
•  Cross-filtering: clicking a region bar on Overview filters all other visuals to that region.
•  Drill-through: right-click any substation name on any page → Substation Detail.
•  Bookmarks: Chart View / Table View toggle on Overview page.
•  Navigation: left-side button panel for page navigation.
•  Conditional formatting: reversed green/red for YoY Growth (energy: growth = cost concern), red background for anomalous readings, data bars on consumption columns, icon set for quality_flag.
Mobile Layout
•  Create mobile layout for Grid Overview: 3 KPI cards at top, regional bar chart below. No detail tables.
Acceptance Criteria
•  5 report pages created with correct visual hierarchy (headline → trend → detail).
•  Slicers synced across pages. Cross-filtering configured.
•  Drill-through to Substation Detail works from any page.
•  Conditional formatting applied (at least 3 patterns).
•  Mobile layout created for Overview page.
•  All 20+ measures visible in appropriate visuals.
Modules Referenced: BI4 (design principles, visual selection, slicers, drill-through, conditional formatting, bookmarks, AI visuals, mobile layout).
Phase 5: Row-Level Security and Governance
Deliverables
•  Dynamic RLS: create the “Regional Access” role with filter [user_email] = USERPRINCIPALNAME() on rls_user_region_mapping.
•  Verify bi-directional relationship: rls_user_region_mapping[region] ↔ dim_substations[region].
•  Assign all report users to the “Regional Access” role.
•  Test: “View as role” with 3 users — single-region, multi-region, all-regions.
•  Test edge case: user with no mapping rows sees blank visuals (no data).
•  Apply sensitivity label: “Confidential” on sm_grid_analytics.
•  Endorse: set sm_grid_analytics to “Certified.”
•  Verify lineage: open lineage view and confirm the chain from data source through semantic model to reports.
Acceptance Criteria
•  Dynamic RLS working: NORTH manager sees only NORTH data across all 5 pages.
•  CFO sees all 4 regions (4 rows in mapping table).
•  No-mapping user sees blank visuals, not an error, not all data.
•  Sensitivity label applied. Label inherits to reports.
•  Certified badge visible on sm_grid_analytics in the Fabric portal.
Modules Referenced: BI6 (dynamic RLS, OLS, testing, sensitivity labels, endorsement, lineage).
Phase 6: Distribution — App, Subscriptions, Embedding
Deliverables
•  Create Power BI App: “NordGrid Grid Analytics.”
•  Navigation sections: “Operational” (Grid Overview, Regional Analysis, Meter Anomalies) and “Executive” (Executive Summary).
•  Audience: GridOperationsTeam + ExecutiveTeam security groups.
•  Create a dashboard: pin 3 KPI cards + trend line from Grid Overview + regional bar from Regional Analysis.
•  Configure email subscriptions: daily Grid Overview to operations team (08:00 AM), weekly Executive Summary to CFO (Monday 08:00 AM). Verify RLS is respected per subscriber.
•  Embed the Grid Overview report in a Teams channel tab.
•  Publish the App.
Acceptance Criteria
•  App published with branded navigation (2 sections).
•  Dashboard created with pinned tiles from 2 report pages.
•  At least 2 email subscriptions configured with correct schedule and format.
•  Teams tab embedded and interactive (slicers, drill-through work).
•  RLS applies in all distribution channels: App, dashboard, subscription, Teams.
Modules Referenced: BI5 (Apps, dashboards, subscriptions, Teams embedding, Copilot).
Phase 7: Verification and Architecture Review
Verification Checklist
•  Measure accuracy: Total Consumption kWh in a card with no filters matches the known grand total. Compare to a direct SQL query on the Gold fact table.
•  Time intelligence: YTD Consumption for March 2026 equals January + February + March consumption. Verify by placing individual months in a matrix.
•  Comparison: Consumption % of Total across all 4 regions in a matrix sums to 100% (or very close due to rounding).
•  Ranking: Substation Rank = 1 is the highest-consumption substation. Verified by sorting a table by Total Consumption kWh DESC.
•  RLS: NORTH manager’s Total Consumption kWh < the unfiltered grand total. The difference equals the other 3 regions’ contribution.
•  Drill-through: right-click a substation on the Overview page, drill through to Substation Detail, verify the detail page filters to that exact substation.
•  Subscription: check email delivery. Verify the snapshot contains data filtered by the subscriber’s RLS role.
•  Mobile: open the Grid Overview on a phone (or phone simulator). Verify KPI cards are readable and the regional chart renders correctly.
Architecture Review Questions
Answer these questions. They are the questions a senior BI architect would ask:

•  The engineering team adds a new column (carbon_intensity_kg) to the fact table. What do you need to change in the semantic model? In the report? (Answer: add the column to the model, write a new DAX measure, add to a visual. No relationship changes.)

•  A new region (CENTRAL) comes online. What changes are needed for RLS? For the report? (Answer: add CENTRAL rows to rls_user_region_mapping for authorized users. No DAX changes. No report changes — the region slicer auto-discovers new values.)
•  The CFO asks for a quarterly PDF sent to the board. Which feature do you use? (Answer: email subscription with PDF format, or a paginated report for pixel-perfect board formatting.)

•  A slicer on the Regional Analysis page shows 50K substation values. Performance is poor. How do you fix it? (Answer: replace the substation name slicer with a region slicer + drill-through. Or use a searchable dropdown. Never put high-cardinality fields in list slicers.)

•  An analyst reports that YoY Growth % shows BLANK for January 2020. Why? (Answer: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR shifts to January 2019, which has no data in dim_date if your range starts at 2020. The denominator is BLANK, and DIVIDE returns 0 or BLANK. Expected behavior.)

•  You discover a report connected to a non-certified semantic model that shows different totals. How do you prevent this? (Answer: endorse sm_grid_analytics as Certified. Communicate that all reports must use the Certified model. Use lineage view to find unauthorized models.)
Completion Checklist
Business Intelligence Track Complete

You have built a complete enterprise BI solution in Microsoft Fabric. Not a demo. Not a prototype. A production system with a DirectLake semantic model, 20+ DAX measures, 5 interactive report pages, dynamic Row-Level Security, governed distribution via App, scheduled subscriptions, Teams embedding, and mobile access.

You understand not just how to write DAX and place visuals, but how to think about the analytical layer. You understand filter context — the engine that makes every measure dynamically adapt to every user interaction. You understand the star schema — the structure that makes relationships, slicers, and drill-through work correctly. You understand governance — the security, labeling, and endorsement that makes the solution trustworthy at enterprise scale.

The BI solution you built today is Version 1. Business requirements evolve. New KPIs emerge (carbon intensity, demand response savings). New audiences appear (regulators, external partners via Embed API). New data sources arrive (weather correlation, solar generation). The skills you have learned in this track give you the foundation to extend, optimize, and evolve the solution for whatever the business needs next.
What Comes Next

•  DW Track (Track 2) — Build the Gold star schema yourself with T-SQL stored procedures, MERGE patterns, SCD Type 2, and Pipeline orchestration. Understand the data layer you are building reports on.
•  DE Track (Track 1) — Go deeper: build the full Bronze → Silver → Gold pipeline with PySpark and Spark SQL. Own the entire data lifecycle.
•  Real-Time Track (Track 3) — Add live SCADA dashboards with Eventstreams, KQL queries, and auto-refreshing Real-Time Dashboards.
•  Administration Track (Track 6) — Implement deployment pipelines (Dev → Test → Prod), Git integration, capacity management, and platform-level monitoring.
Congratulations.
From Data Layer Literacy to Enterprise BI Solution.
9 Modules · 90 Lessons · One Complete Analytical Experience.
